title: Prompt Engineering for Vision Models
concept: Vision prompt engineering involves crafting text templates that maximize zero-shot matching accuracy in multimodal models.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What loss function is used to align image and text encoders in CLIP?
exercises.explain: Correct! Contrastive loss pulls correct image-text pairs close and pushes incorrect pairs away.

cards.title: Context is key
cards.body: If you ask a friend 'find the seal', they might look for a marine animal or a stamp on a letter. If you say 'find a photo of a seal swimming in water', the context makes it clear. Vision prompt engineering provides this vital context to models.

cards.title: Vision Prompting
cards.body: Prompt Engineering for Vision Models is the practice of designing specific text templates (like 'a photo of a [class]' or 'a close-up of a [class]') to generate target text vectors. The choice of prompt template has a massive impact on zero-shot accuracy.

cards.title: Giving a role to an actor
cards.body: Think of prompt templates as costume choices for an actor. If you just label a class 'dog', the actor wears normal clothes. If you wrap it as 'a rendering of a dog in a cartoon style', the actor gets the exact costume needed to match a stylized image.

cards.title: Ensembling prompts
cards.body: Instead of using one prompt, engineers often use a collection (ensemble) of prompts like 'a photo of a [class]', 'a satellite view of [class]', and 'a black and white photo of a [class]', then average their text embeddings to improve robustness.

cards.title: Raw class names are rarely optimal
cards.body: You might think passing raw class names like 'cat' or 'car' to the text encoder is best. In reality, because CLIP was pre-trained on full captions, raw single words are out-of-distribution. Using templates like 'a photo of a [class]' typically improves accuracy by 5% to 10%!

cards.title: Prompt Variations
cards.body: Comparing raw label prompting with templated prompting:

left.text:
Text Input: 'guacamole'
Very short, out-of-distribution
Poor zero-shot accuracy

right.text:
Text Input: 'a photo of guacamole, a type of food'
Rich context match
High zero-shot accuracy

exercises.prompt: Why do raw single-word labels (like 'dog') perform worse in CLIP than templated prompts (like 'a photo of a dog')?
exercises.explain: CLIP was pre-trained on web captions which are full descriptive sentences. Providing a sentence template matches this training distribution.

options.text: Because CLIP was pre-trained on full caption sentences, making single words out-of-distribution
Because single words cannot be converted to vectors
Because the text encoder only accepts inputs longer than ten words
Because single words cause the model to crash

exercises.prompt: True or false: Prompt ensembling in vision models means averaging the text embeddings of multiple different templates to improve matching accuracy.
exercises.explain: True. Averaging embeddings from multiple templates (like 'a photo of a...', 'a rendering of a...') increases stability and accuracy.
Prompt engineering is the design and optimization of input prompts to guide model outputs.
exercises.sentence: Wrapping raw class names in context templates to improve classification is called prompt ___.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is an example of an ensembled prompt template?
exercises.explain: Averaging multiple templated sentence vectors represents prompt ensembling in vision models.

options.text: Averaging the embeddings of 'a photo of a [class]' and 'a zoomed-in view of the [class]'
Inputting the class name 'apple' directly to the image encoder
Passing the image through five different convolutional kernels

exercises.prompt: You are building a model to classify microscopical medical cells. Which prompt design is most likely to yield high zero-shot accuracy with CLIP?

options.text: A template like 'a microscopic image of a [class] cell', which matches the specific domain of the photos.
A general template like 'a funny cartoon of a [class]'.
The raw class name like 'lymphocyte' with no templates.

exercises.explain: Exactly! By incorporating domain-specific descriptors like 'microscopic image of a... cell', you guide CLIP to match the specialized visual texture of medical photography. This is key to successful vision prompting. Next, let's review our entire advanced classification toolkit in the capstone!
